pages.name: The Veska Reveal — Reading the Chops

text.content:
The set-piece scene where Veska Maelan reads the recovered evidence and names the people behind it. The single most important investigative beat in Phase 3 — everything afterward unfolds from what Veska tells the party here.

Setup

The party brings their evidence to Veska's notary office on Whalemarket Quay. Depending on travel approach, the meeting happens differently:

  • Quiet Pursuit: An evening session, hours of careful work. Veska closes the office, brings up the upstairs lamp, makes tea twice. The reading takes most of a night.
  • Commercial Cover: Two daytime sessions, second and third visits. Veska works in business hours only; the readings are interrupted by walk-in clients she cannot turn away without arousing suspicion. Two to three hours per session.
  • Official Delegation: Veska is gone. Her former apprentice Hella Voren (after the agreed phrase is spoken) hands the party a sealed packet of Veska's notes and chop-rubbings. The reveal is read silently in the party's lodging, by lamplight, in roughly the same words Veska would have said.

Read aloud (in Veska's voice if present, in the silent voice of her notes if not)

"Set them on the table for me. The escrow first. Then the coin. Then the lacquered tube and the seed pouch. The ledger fragment goes here, between the lamp and my elbow. Yes. That is how I will read them."

(She works in silence for some time. Lenses on, lenses off, lenses on. Tracing chops with one careful finger, holding rubbings to lamplight, comparing hands. When she is ready she does not announce it; she simply begins.)

"The escrow's house seal is the firm's, the public one. That tells you nothing. Anyone with the firm's wax could press it.

The Hardby clearing-chop in the lower-left margin was applied by Solen Mereth. He runs the bond-loading office at the Hardby branch. I know his hand the way you know the shape of your own kitchen at night. He will not have signed it elsewhere — he is too careful — but I have three other documents in my files where his chop sits next to his hand on the same page, and I will give you those for comparison.

The marginal endorsement — Hold for V., or his agent of record, against presentation — is in a different hand. Smaller. Older. I have seen it three times in the last four years and I have never been able to put a name to it from a single document. But."

(She lifts the escrow into the lamplight. Holds it there for a long moment.)

"If you ask me whose hand this is, and you give me until the end of this week to lay it next to the only three other examples I have, I will tell you it is Tarlith Vhal Sereth's. And if I am wrong, I am wrong by one office. He is the senior partner at Cindren and Vhal Rel Astra who has not signed a letter at his own desk in eleven years. The firm pretends he is unwell. He is not unwell. He is hiding what he writes."

(She sets the escrow down. Picks up the coin.)

"Now look at the countermark. Tower above three waves. The firm has seven of these stamps, one to each office. They are supposed to be unique to their offices and identical within the firm. This one — see the chip on the lower-right wave — is the Hardby stamp. I know it because I notarized its replacement in 579 CY. Solen brought me a new stamp for the firm's records and asked me to certify the old one as destroyed. I notarized the certification. I assumed the old one was destroyed. I assumed wrong. The old stamp, the chipped one, the one this coin was weighed in on — that stamp is in the upstairs strongroom of the Hardby branch tonight, and it has been there for four years.

The vellum strip with the four words. Harbor before throne. That is older language. It is from the Aerdi Concordat of the Bay, the imperial settlement that established the harbor commissions. It says, in plainer Common: when there is no king sitting and confirmed, the harbor councils run themselves and run their cities. It is a clause about timing. Whoever wrote those four words on that vellum is telling someone — telling Solen, I think — that the seating of a harbor council before a throne is settled is the lever that will work."

(She lifts the seed pouch, holds it to the lamp, sets it down.)

"The seeds are display, not stock. The treatment to make them look fresh is alchemical. Whoever bought this pouch did not need to grow grain — they needed grain to look grown. The dry barony Vandrell was carrying these for is in South Province, but the receipts the conspiracy has written on grain it never grew clear through Hardby's weighbridge. Tamsin Moraven was within a month of being able to prove it."

(She closes her ledgers. Folds her hands on the desk.)

"You came here for names. I will give you four.

One. Solen Mereth, head of bond-loading at the Hardby branch. He chopped the escrow. He kept the defect-stamp. He runs the second set of books at the branch level. He is the operational lever.

Two. Tarlith Vhal Sereth, senior partner at Rel Astra. He wrote the marginal endorsement. He is the one whose name a court must hear if any of this is to mean anything outside this room.

Three. Anver Resh, freelance broker, formerly Solen's clerk. You will find him at the Coopered Wreck most evenings. He hires people for things the firm cannot put in writing. He is the man Sarth was given by.

Four. There is a fourth. I do not have the name. I have a hand — the hand that wrote one further annotation in the last set of records I saw, two months before they stopped letting documents I could see cross my desk. That hand is not Sereth's. It is not Solen's. It is not Resh's. It is not anyone in Hardby. It is the hand that the firm answers to. I do not know whose it is. I will tell you, before you leave for Rel Astra, the things I do know about how that hand writes the letter ‘E.' That is all I have. Make of it what you can."

What the party leaves with

  • Names: Solen Mereth, Tarlith Vhal Sereth, Anver Resh.
  • Veska's prepared notarial opinion identifying the Hardby chop, the defect-stamp, and (with comparison documents) the Sereth marginal endorsement. This is the document that turns the recovered evidence into a legally admissible package in Aerdi or Yeomanry court.
  • The fourth-hand "E." observation. Veska does not name Eclavdra. She cannot. She knows only that there is a fourth hand the firm answers to, that this fourth hand writes its sole annotation as a single capital initial, and that the letter is "E." She will give the party her observations on how the letter is formed — three small details about the loop and the serif and the angle of the descender — that may, much later, let the party recognize the hand again. Phase 3 does not name Eclavdra. Veska does not know to name her. Hold this thread.
  • The harbor-before-throne reading. Veska gives the party the operational frame for the phrase. They will need additional Hardby investigation (Zoria Weis, the Gynarchy's harbor commission registry) to confirm what the conspiracy is positioning toward, but the frame is set: seat the harbor before the throne.

What Veska does next

Quiet Pursuit: Veska continues to work in Hardby. She sends the party off with everything she has and tells them she will be here when they next need her. She is here when they next need her — through Phase 4 and beyond.

Commercial Cover: Veska finishes her opinion on the second day, hands it to the party in business hours, and warns them she will close the office for "a quiet week" once they leave Hardby. She does. She survives.

Official Delegation: Veska, who fled before the party arrived, is in a coastal village two weeks east. Hella Voren passes the party one final letter from her: "If you find Tarlith Vhal Sereth, ask him about the autumn of 571. He will know what you mean. I will know also, when you write to me about it."

Outcome by travel approach

What the party finds when they pursue the Tamsin Moraven thread depends on which travel approach they chose at the end of Phase 2. None of the three is a failure state; all three carry weight.

Quiet Pursuit — Live Recovery in Hardby

Where she is

A warehouse on Black Cog Lane in the harbor district. Owned through the shell company "Greenwall & Mast" (which Zoria Weis or Veska can confirm as a Cindren & Vhal cutout). Two-story stone-and-timber warehouse, an attached caretaker's residence, an old loading dock onto the canal at the rear.

The setup

Tamsin is held in a small first-floor room that was once a clerk's office — single window (barred), wooden door (locked from outside), straw mattress, water jug, chamber pot, no light after sunset. She has been here twelve days. She is alive but tired and underfed. She has not been physically harmed; her captors have been told to keep her until further instruction. She has spent her time memorizing the daily schedule of the warehouse.

Captors

  • Three hired guards (use SRD Thug) on rotating shifts, two on at any time. They are paid by the week and not personally invested. They do not know who Tamsin is or why she is held; they were told "a difficult client." They will fight if attacked but will surrender if outnumbered or cornered.
  • One Cindren-aligned overseer, Reyna Worth (use SRD Veteran), who is the only one who knows what Tamsin is. She arrives daily at the third bell after dawn for a brief check, leaves by the fourth. She is loyal to the firm, will not surrender, and will try to kill Tamsin if she suspects rescue is imminent. Resolving Worth before she can give the order is the rescue's central tactical problem.

The rescue

Best approach: enter at night when only the two thug-guards are on watch and Worth is at home. Stealth approach to the back loading-dock (DC 14), pick the rear lock (DC 15), neutralize the two guards (combat or stealth), open Tamsin's room (DC 12 lock).

Worth-mitigation: if the party can arrange Worth's day to be disrupted (a fire two streets over, a fake summons from the harbormaster, a poisoned breakfast — Castrian or Resh can both arrange any of these) so that she does not arrive for her morning check, the party has clear access for at least one full day before the firm realizes Tamsin is gone.

If the party assaults during a Worth-present hour: more dangerous. Worth will try to kill Tamsin within her first round; the party must reach Tamsin's room before Worth does. Initiative matters.

After

Tamsin needs three days of rest, food, and quiet before she is fit to travel. Castrian's house can hide her, or Trina's letter to a coastal innkeeper can house her, or — if the party is being chased — the sculptor's workshop's cellar can hide her for a night. She must be out of Hardby within a week or the firm will find her again.

Live Tamsin is the strongest possible Phase 4 witness. She has been keeping mental notes on what she heard in the warehouse — names mentioned by Worth, papers carried in and out, the rhythm of the operation. She can corroborate Veska's reading and add operational detail no one else can.

Commercial Cover — Live Recovery East of Hardby

Where she is

The Whitemoor Estate, a former wine-house turned coastal residence, two days east on the coast road. Owned through the same shell company. The estate sits on a small headland with a private dock; the coast road approaches it through a stand of pine wood. There is one main house (two stories, eight rooms), a stable, a former wine-pressing barn, and a small dock on a sheltered cove.

The setup

Tamsin was moved here four days ago when the firm received Hesren Vesh's recall message and decided that the Loftwick-side investigation was real. She is held in an upstairs bedchamber. She has freedom of the upstairs corridor (door locked at night) and is fed properly. She has been told nothing about why she is held but has guessed most of it. She has been at the estate long enough to know the house and grounds.

Captors

  • Six hired guards (SRD Bandit Captain and five Thugs), more competent than the Hardby crew. The captain is a contractor named Olo Crask, retired Aerdi marine, professional, careful. He is paid handsomely, will fight to the last, but is not personally murderous — he will not kill Tamsin without explicit order, and the order has not been given.
  • One Cindren overseer, Reyna Worth (the same Worth as the Hardby case; she travels), present at the estate one day in three. The other two days she is in Hardby coordinating the firm's response.
  • One sealed-orders courier who arrives every five days from Hardby with new instruction. The next courier is due two days after the party can reasonably reach the estate. If the party arrives ahead of the next courier, the standing order is "hold safely." If they arrive after, the new order may be "move further east" or "dispose of asset" depending on what the firm has learned.

The rescue

This is a more substantial operation than the Hardby warehouse. The estate is defended; the party will need either reconnaissance + careful infiltration, or a frontal assault with planning. Castrian can arrange travel to the estate's vicinity (a former lover of his, a wine merchant, owns the next headland over and will host the party for a night). Veska can identify the captain Crask by reputation and brief the party on what to expect.

Best approach: arrive ahead of the next courier, scout for a day, infiltrate by sea (the dock is the weakest point and Crask has fewer guards stationed there) on the second night.

After

Tamsin is in better condition than the Hardby version (she has been fed and housed properly), but the journey back is longer and the firm is more alert. The party returns Tamsin to Hardby quietly and houses her at Castrian's for two days before extracting her to Loftwick. The trip back to Loftwick is dangerous; consider a sea passage to Saltmarsh and overland from there.

Live Tamsin is, again, the strongest Phase 4 witness. She is also visibly grateful, which lands as a quiet beat for the party — she did not believe she would be recovered.

Official Delegation — Body Recovered, Letter Smuggled

What happened

Tamsin was murdered by Reyna Worth at the Black Cog Lane warehouse three days before the party arrived in Hardby. The order came from Solen Mereth on confirmation that an accredited Yeomanry delegation had filed for entry. Tamsin was killed quickly (a single thrust); her body was placed in a barrel and dumped in the harbor; a fisherman recovered the barrel two days later. The Hardby city watch certified the death as "harbor incident, foul play probable, jurisdiction limited."

What the party recovers

  • The body. The party can claim it through the watch (the accredited delegation has standing for foreign-national death claims). Tamsin can be returned to Loftwick for burial; Vellin and Ailen can lay her to rest.
  • Her last letter. A warehouse worker at the Black Cog Lane facility — a young woman named Lerra Voss, who served Tamsin food in the warehouse for the twelve days she was held — smuggled out a single folded paper Tamsin had written. Lerra has been afraid to deliver it; when the party arrives in Hardby and a known Yeomanry delegation is in the city, Lerra approaches Hella Voren (Veska's apprentice) and asks her to pass it on. Hella does. Lerra herself disappears within a week of giving the letter; her family does not know what happened to her. The party may, in Phase 4 or beyond, choose to investigate.

The letter (read aloud or printed as a handout)

Vellin and Ailen — if you read this, I am not coming home.

I want you to know that I knew what I was doing when I asked the questions, and I would ask them again. The grain that was paid for never existed. The men who paid for it knew it never existed and bought it anyway because the paperwork is a kind of weapon, and they wanted the weapon. I followed the weapon back through three counting houses to the firm, and the firm to a name in Rel Astra, and the name to a man who has been dead, on paper, for nine years. I do not know who the man is. I know only that he is the one I was asking about when they came for me.

Vellin — there is a small lacquered box in the bottom of my wardrobe under the spare linen. Inside it, my filings on the South Province grain. Take them to the Hardby weighmaster Joren Krill, no — to Caelith Dunivar in Loftwick. Joren is not safe. Caelith is. He will know what to do with the filings. He may already know.

Ailen — your work has been the great quiet joy of my life. I have not told you this often enough. The portrait you carved of your grandmother is on my desk. I look at it every morning. If I am gone, take it back; do not let it sit in this office.

I love you both very much. I am sorry to have left you with this.

— T.

What this means for Phase 4

Without live Tamsin, the Phase 4 indictment is built on documents alone. It is still viable — Veska's notarial opinion plus the recovered branch records (via subpoena) plus Tamsin's letter (admissible as testamentary evidence in Aerdi court) plus the body itself (with coronial findings of foul play) is a substantial package. But the loss is real. Treat this beat with the gravity it deserves. Vellin and Ailen carrying Tamsin's coffin home from Hardby is a cutscene that the GM should let the table sit with for as long as the table needs.

The set-piece scene where the party confronts Anver "Tarsh" Resh in person. Available on Quiet Pursuit (best case) or Commercial Cover (more guarded). Not directly available on Official Delegation; on that path, see the GM note at the end.

The Coopered Wreck

A long, low, coal-smoke-dark sailors' tavern on the harbor district edge — three blocks from the wharves, two from the harbor wall, one from the rope-walks. The proprietor is Olfard Trent, a one-eyed retired bosun in his sixties. The taproom is a single deep room with a long bar down one side and three back booths on the opposite wall. The leftmost back booth is Tarsh's by long custom; Olfard does not seat anyone else there.

Patronage is mostly working harbor men: stevedores, junior officers off shore leave, dock-warden retirees, the occasional retired marine. The room runs busy from the seventh bell of evening through the eleventh, then thins. Tarsh arrives between the seventh and eighth bells most evenings he is in town and stays through the tenth or eleventh.

The approach

The party walks in. They will not be the first strangers who have ever been in the Coopered Wreck, but they will be the first strangers in a long while who walked directly to the back booth. Olfard will watch them from the bar. Tarsh will see them coming and stay seated.

Tarsh's first move is conversational, not violent. He is a broker; he assumes the party is here to hire him until they prove otherwise. "Sit down," he says, gesturing to the bench across from him. "Two of you. Tell me what brings you. The other three or four can stand at the bar. Olfard will pour them."

This is genuine. He believes himself to be safe in his own booth, in Olfard's house, with at least one bodyguard within thirty feet (on Quiet Pursuit, no bodyguard; on Commercial Cover, one within a stone's throw outside the tavern's back door).

The conversation

Tarsh leads. He asks the party who recommended him. He asks what they need done. He asks how they pay.

The party can play this two ways:

1. Maintain the broker pretext (Deception)

The party pretends to be hiring him. This buys time and lets them ask questions in a frame Tarsh accepts. Useful for getting him to talk about how the firm pays him, who his Hardby contacts are, what he charges for what kinds of work. Insight DC 14: Tarsh is genuinely available for hire and is not currently on alert. Deception DC 16 to maintain pretext past five minutes — too many specific questions about Cindren & Vhal will tip him.

If the pretext breaks: Tarsh stands smoothly, says, "I think you are not here to hire me. I think you should leave my booth." The party can then either escalate (see Confrontation below) or back off (Tarsh slips out the back within the hour and the party has lost the chance — though Veska's documents still name him).

2. Direct confrontation

The party shows him a piece of recovered evidence — a chop he applied, a name he transferred, anything from Veska's package that names him in writing — and tells him plainly what they want.

Tarsh's reaction is not panic. He sets his pipe down, looks at the document carefully, lets out a long thin breath of pipe-smoke through his nose, and says: "Well. That is a paper I would have preferred not to be holding."

Then he listens. He weighs. He answers questions in short careful sentences.

What he gives up

If the party has appropriate leverage (a smoking-gun document, witness testimony, or simply outnumbers him with witnesses present), Resh yields what he knows in exchange for a one-way passport out of Hardby and a written guarantee that his name does not appear in the eventual indictment. Caelith will honor this in writing if asked.

What he tells the party (in roughly this order, as they ask):

  1. Solen Mereth. "Yes. He runs the second set of books out of the Hardby branch. Has done since 1580. I was his clerk when he started. He kept me on as outside contractor when I left."
  2. Tarlith Vhal Sereth. "The senior partner who signs off. I have never met him. No one outside the inner senior partners has, in eleven years. I have carried two sealed packets to Solen on Sereth's instruction. I can describe Sereth's seal in detail; I have never seen his face."
  3. Tamsin Moraven. Tarsh confirms her location based on the travel approach: warehouse on Black Cog Lane (Quiet Pursuit), the Whitemoor Estate two days east (Commercial Cover), or — if asked late and Tamsin is already dead — confirms it was Solen who ordered the killing and Worth who did it.
  4. His payment chain. The firm pays him in untraceable cash, delivered weekly by a courier who is a different person each time. He suspects the couriers are run by Worth. He does not know how the firm pays Worth.
  5. The Rel Astra contact — refused. Tarsh will not name his counterpart in Rel Astra, even with leverage. "That man," he says, "is not someone I will trade for a passport. Take what I have given you and go. If you press, I will say nothing else, and you will have made an enemy of me, and I would prefer not to be your enemy."

If combat starts

Tarsh fights only as a last resort and only to escape. His full statblock is in the Phase 3 NPCs compendium (CR 6, hand crossbow, smoke pellets, Cunning Action, Uncanny Dodge). On Quiet Pursuit he has no bodyguards in the tavern. On Commercial Cover, one bodyguard outside the back door (use Bandit Captain). On either approach, if combat starts in the Coopered Wreck, two paid harbor-district enforcers (Thugs) drinking at the bar will defend Tarsh while he exits through Olfard's back room.

Olfard himself does not fight. He stays at the bar, watches, and remembers everything.

If the party kills Tarsh

Strongly discouraged. See his dossier (Hardby NPCs → Anver Resh) for full consequences. Short version: the party loses a major source of corroboration and intel, and the firm assumes a third party is in play, which destabilizes Phase 4 in unpredictable ways.

Official Delegation note

If the party is on the Official Delegation approach, Tarsh has been moved upcountry and the Coopered Wreck confrontation does not happen. The party identifies him through paper trails (Veska's notes, the C&V vault if breached, Hesren's notebook if Hesren is recovered). They never confront him in person on this approach. However — if the party explicitly returns to Hardby in Phase 4 or later, Tarsh may have surfaced again at a different establishment, and a confrontation can be arranged then. He is not gone forever; he is only gone for now.

pages.name: Harbor Before Throne — The Operational Reveal

text.content:
The investigative beat that takes the Phase 2 slogan and turns it into operational law. Best run in Hardby, after Veska's reveal, with Zoria Weis as the bridge to the Gynarchy's harbor-commission registry. Read this carefully — the reveal is meant to clarify the conspiracy's eastern objective without yet naming the candidate harbor commissioners or the Aerdy claimant. That is Phase 4.

Where the reveal happens

The Office of Mercantile Records, on the Council steps in the Old Quarter. Zoria Weis works at the southwest desk, third in from the door. She receives the party in the late morning of an ordinary working day, having confirmed the agreed phrase ("The autumn ledger"). She brings them to a small reading room at the back of the Office where the harbor-commission appointment registry is kept.

What the registry shows

Read aloud or paraphrase as Zoria shows the party the open volume:

"Rel Astra's harbor commission has seven seats. They are filled by old Aerdi imperial appointment, four hundred years of practice. Three of the seats — the First, the Third, and the Fifth — are filled by the old houses, by appointment that the seated Overking confirms when he is seated and confirmed himself. The other four are appointed by the harbormaster of Rel Astra and the city's mercantile guilds. Those four are filled now and have been for years; nothing strange there.

The three old-house seats are different. They are filled now, on paper. But the three current holders are dead."

(She turns the page.)

"The First Seat: Lord Hessar Olm, deceased 580 CY of natural causes, no successor formally seated.

The Third Seat: Lady Mirin Vesh, deceased 581 CY in a fall on her own staircase, no successor formally seated.

The Fifth Seat: Master Ervan Dale, deceased 582 CY of a brief illness, no successor formally seated.

The seats sit vacant. By Aerdi practice, the city's harbor business has been conducted by the four mercantile-guild seats only, with the three old-house seats noted as ‘pending confirmation.' This is unusual but not yet illegal — the law allows up to two years of vacancy before action is required. The first vacancy is two years old this autumn. The second is fourteen months old. The third is six months old.

The Aerdy succession is, as everyone knows, a question. The Overking is old. There are three claimants seriously in play and a half-dozen whose mothers think they should be. When a new Overking is seated, he must confirm the three old-house harbor commissioners of Rel Astra before he can collect harbor revenue from the city. Confirmation requires the harbor commissioners to exist. Currently they do not.

Whoever has positioned candidates to be appointed to those three vacancies before a new Overking is named will hand the new Overking three pre-confirmed votes on the harbor commission, controlling the eastern revenue. Or, if those three candidates vote against confirmation, the new Overking has no harbor commission to confirm at all, and Rel Astra's harbor revenue stops feeding the imperial treasury until the matter is resolved. Either way, whoever owns those three seats owns the eastern question."

(She closes the volume.)

"That is what your slogan means. Harbor before throne, in Aerdi practical law, is the timing window before a new Overking is seated, in which old-house harbor commissioners can be appointed by the existing protocol — by the senior partners of Rel Astra's old commercial families, voting in convocation. Cindren and Vhal Rel Astra is one of those old families."

What the party should leave with

  • The conspiracy is positioning to seat three candidates on Rel Astra's harbor commission before the next Aerdy Overking is named.
  • The three current vacancies were created on schedule: each old-house commissioner died on a date that opened the seat in time for the seating window. Two years, fourteen months, six months. The deaths are not random. (GM: Mirin Vesh's "fall" was Reyna Worth's work; Ervan Dale's "brief illness" was Reln Pollow's understudy in Rel Astra; Hessar Olm's "natural causes" were natural and the conspiracy got lucky on the first one. The party will not learn this in Phase 3.)
  • Cindren & Vhal Rel Astra is one of the old commercial families with a vote in the appointment convocation. Tarlith Vhal Sereth is, by virtue of his rank, one of the senior partners eligible to vote. The Sereth signature on the Vandrell escrow is therefore not just a corrupt signature — it is the signature of a man positioning himself to seat three commissioners on the harbor of Rel Astra.
  • The party does not yet know which three candidates the conspiracy intends to seat. Phase 3 stops here. Phase 4 names them.

What this clarifies for the table

The Phase 1/2 slogan was atmospheric. Phase 3 makes it operational: the conspiracy is, very specifically, racing the Aerdy succession. They want their people seated on Rel Astra's harbor commission before a new Overking can stop them. The Phase 4 question becomes: who are the three candidates, and who is seating them, and how soon.

The party should leave Phase 3 understanding that they cannot afford to wait. Whatever Phase 4 looks like, it is on a clock that is not their clock — it is the Aerdy succession's clock, and the conspiracy is ahead of them.

What this does not yet clarify

  • Who the three candidate commissioners are. Phase 4 thread.
  • Which Aerdy claimant the conspiracy supports (or whether they support none and want only to control the eastern revenue regardless of who wins). Phase 4 thread.
  • Why "E." is involved. The conspiracy as the party understands it now is mercantile and political — three harbor seats, eastern revenue, succession leverage. Eclavdra's interest is older and stranger and not visible in Phase 3's frame. This is the Drow lineage thread. Hold it for much later.
